question: Should the prd-to-product skill kit add a NEW SIBLING SKILL (design-handoff-adopt) to ingest Claude Design handoff bundles (HTML/CSS/JS prototypes + an existing token-CSS design system + screenshots + sometimes a PRD) and codify them into the kit's conventions? Alternatives: (B) a second mode inside the existing design-system-bootstrap skill; (C) no new capability, handle each bundle as a one-off; (D) defer until a second bundle appears. The kit is a single-developer workspace of single-purpose, markdown-only skills that don't auto-compose (chained by hand; an /idea-to-product orchestrator is proposed but unbuilt). The mechanical part is token transcription (parse :root CSS custom properties into a two-tier tokens.css, preserve oklch() exactly, Tailwind off); the judgment parts are multi-aesthetic reconciliation and pixel-perfect component recreation. Claude Design ships its own README instructing implementing agents. Pressure-test: is 'extract/transcribe, don't generate' a repeatable skill procedure or bespoke judgment? Does a 4th node deepen the chain-composition weakness? What's the honest ceiling?
title: Adopting Claude Design handoffs: new skill, mode, or neither?

advisors.name: The Skeptic
advisors.response:
"Extract, don't generate" is not a skill — it's a transcription chore wearing a skill costume. What actually varies run to run: which aesthetic is a theme variant vs. dead-end; whether a screenshot is canonical or scratch; how a bespoke component decomposes into token-wired primitives; what the PRD says to keep. Every one is human judgment Claude Design's own README already steers. The repeatable 20% (copy :root tokens verbatim into two-tier tokens.css, flip Tailwind off) is a 30-line slash command, not a fourth skill.

Three failure modes nobody's naming:

1. Fidelity trap. "Preserve values exactly" sounds safe, but the kit's two-tier token shape will not be isomorphic to a 40-token oklch/motion/font system. The moment you map their structure onto yours, you've mutated it — and a lossy "adopt" is strictly worse than just using the bundle as-is, because now there are two sources of truth drifting.
2. N=1. You're designing a reconciliation procedure for "multiple aesthetics" from exactly one bundle. Build the abstraction now and you'll hard-code this bundle's quirks as "the format."
3. Chain debt. A fourth non-composing node in a chain whose headline weakness is non-composition.

Honest ceiling: reliably copies tokens and sets a flag. Everything distinctive stays human. Verdict: D, leaning C. Defer. Do the next bundle by hand against the README, log what was genuinely mechanical, and only then decide if there's a skill-shaped residue.

advisors.name: The Architect
advisors.response:
"Adopt vs. bootstrap" is the wrong axis to optimize. Strip it down: what you actually have is a tool-handoff problem. Claude Design already emits a README telling agents how to implement the bundle. The kit's job is not to re-implement that README as a skill — it's to translate the bundle into the kit's conventions so the rest of the kit (rules, docs, write guard) can grip it. That's a much smaller, more repeatable thing than "recreate components pixel-perfect."

So decompose the proposed skill. It bundles three jobs of wildly different repeatability:

1. Token transcription — deterministic. Parse :root, preserve values, fold into two-tier tokens.css. This is a real, repeatable procedure. A skill earns its keep here.
2. Multi-aesthetic reconciliation — pure human judgment. No skill makes "are these theme variants or competing directions?" repeatable. The skill can only surface the fork and ask.
3. Pixel-perfect component recreation — that's Claude Design's README's job, not yours. Duplicating it is the trap in the question.

So the honest answer is B, narrowed — an "adopt" mode in design-system-bootstrap, scoped to job #1 only, with job #2 as a prompt and job #3 delegated to the bundle's own README. Bootstrap already owns tokens.css shape; an inverse path (preserve vs. generate, Tailwind off) is one branch, not a fourth chain node.

Smallest experiment that proves the variable: take the real bundle, hand-transcribe its tokens into the kit's tokens.css right now, by hand, timing yourself. If it's mechanical, build the mode. If you keep hitting judgment calls, the skill was never the leverage — the README was. Don't build until that 30-minute test runs.

advisors.name: The Stranger
advisors.response:
"Claude Design," "handoff bundle," "two-tier tokens.css," "the qventus class," "non-destructive write guard," "oklch accents" — these are private vocabulary. The decision is framed as if a reader already agrees that the kit's conventions are worth conforming to, that "single-purpose skills" is a virtue rather than a tax, and that a bundle from Claude Design should be bent into this kit at all. None of that is established for an outsider; it's assumed.

Here is the thing the framing hides. Claude Design already ships a README that tells a coding agent how to implement the bundle. So who is the user of design-handoff-adopt? It is you, one developer, on the maybe-three occasions a year you export a bundle. You are proposing to build, document, regression-test, and maintain a fourth skill whose entire job is to translate an artifact that comes with its own instructions into a house format that exists only because you invented it.

Strip the vocabulary and the question is: "Should I write a permanent tool to reformat a thing that already knows how to be implemented?" Said that plainly, the answer is no.

The honest ceiling confirms it. Token transcription is mechanical (a script, not a skill). Everything with judgment — reconciling multiple aesthetics, pixel-perfect recreation — is exactly what the README already hands to a human-plus-agent. A skill can't make that repeatable; it'll collapse into bespoke judgment every run, which is your pressure-test (1) answered.

Pick D. Do the next bundle as a one-off, write down what was actually mechanical, and only then consider extracting a script — not a skill.

advisors.name: The Long View
advisors.response:
"Extract, don't generate" only compounds if the kit owns the artifact downstream. Three years out, the binding question isn't "can a skill transcribe 40 tokens" — it's who maintains the design system after adoption. Claude Design will keep shipping bundles; its README will keep improving; its output will get richer and more idiosyncratic every release. A skill whose core job is transcription is racing a moving target you don't control. That depreciates fast.

What compounds is the reconciliation layer — the kit's two-tier tokens.css convention, the write guard, the doc structure. That's yours, it's stable, and it's what makes a bundle survive contact with a real codebase over months of edits. So the durable asset isn't "adopt"; it's "the kit has an opinion about how an external design system gets governed once it's in."

That reframes the build. A fourth sibling deepens the non-composition debt: you'd then have four un-orchestrated nodes, and the orchestrator you haven't built gets harder, not easier, because adopt and bootstrap are mutually exclusive entry modes that must be chosen between — which is exactly a routing decision an orchestrator makes. Option B (a mode in bootstrap) pays that debt down: one skill, two entry modes, one place the orchestrator routes to.

Honest ceiling: transcription automates; multi-aesthetic reconciliation and pixel-perfect recreation never will — those stay human every run, README or not. So scope the skill to the part that compounds (governance + token-tier mapping) and let it defer to the README for recreation.

Opportunity cost: building skill #4 is weeks not spent on the orchestrator, which is the actual bottleneck. Build the mode (B). Defer the standalone.

advisors.name: The Veteran
advisors.response:
Without auto-composition, this is a fourth standalone node you invoke by hand on a bundle you already have sitting in a folder. That is the part that should worry you.

The standard playbook says: don't build the inverse skill, write the README's procedure into a slash command and run it. Most "ingest an existing system" tools collapse into bespoke judgment on every run, and you end up maintaining a skill that does less than a careful human reading the Claude Design README. Pressure-test (1) is the whole ballgame: "extract/transcribe, don't generate" is only a real skill where it's mechanical — and exactly one part is mechanical. Parsing :root custom properties into the two-tier tokens.css, preserving oklch() values verbatim, wiring class-based theme variants: that is deterministic, repeatable, and genuinely better than freehand because it can't drift or hallucinate a palette. That part earns its keep.

Everything past the token transcription is judgment that does not compress into a procedure. Multi-aesthetic reconciliation is a design call you make per bundle. Pixel-perfect component recreation from prototypes is the exact thing Claude Design's README already hands the implementing agent — a skill wrapping it adds a node, not capability. That is your honest ceiling: reliable token adoption, advisory everything else.

So the obvious move is right with one correction: this isn't a sibling skill, it's a token-adopt command plus a documented manual recreate step. Build the mechanical 20% as real automation under the existing write guard; leave the 80% as guided prose. Don't spin up a fourth peer to the chain you already admit doesn't compose. Verdict: not (A). A scoped command, closest to (B).

peer_reviews.strongest_why: It decomposes the proposal into three jobs of differing repeatability, assigns each its correct owner (script / human-prompt / README), and proposes a cheap timed hand-transcription test to settle build-vs-defer empirically.
peer_reviews.blind_spot_what: It asserts an unbuilt orchestrator is 'the actual bottleneck' and frames the decision around it, smuggling in a future capability the kit has explicitly chosen not to build to justify mode-over-sibling.
peer_reviews.all_missed: Every response treats the kit-as-target as a given and debates only where the adopt logic lives; none follows the lossy-isomorphism point to its conclusion — if the two-tier tokens.css cannot losslessly hold a 40-token oklch/motion/font system, 'adopt' creates a second drifting source of truth and the right answer may be to NOT ingest at all and use the bundle as-is.

peer_reviews.strongest_why: It cleanly decomposes the proposal into three jobs of different repeatability and proposes the one cheap empirical test (hand-transcribe the real bundle, time it) that actually resolves whether a skill is warranted.
peer_reviews.blind_spot_what: It bets the durable asset is an 'orchestrator' bottleneck and treats adopt/bootstrap as mutually-exclusive routing modes, but the kit's own premise is that skills are chained by hand — so the orchestrator it optimizes for may be a phantom requirement.
peer_reviews.all_missed: Every response treats the bundle's tokens as the thing to ingest, but none confronts the fidelity-trap point that the two-tier shape is non-isomorphic to a 40-token oklch/motion/font system — meaning even the 'mechanical' 20% may be lossy and create two drifting sources of truth, which would argue against adopting the tokens into the kit at all rather than just against building a skill.

peer_reviews.strongest_why: It reframes the axis correctly (tool-handoff, not adopt-vs-bootstrap), decomposes by repeatability into exactly the three jobs, and proposes the cheapest falsifiable experiment — hand-transcribe the real bundle and time it — before committing.
peer_reviews.blind_spot_what: It names the lossy-mapping / two-sources-of-truth drift risk but then ignores it, treating transcription as cleanly mechanical when the kit's two-tier shape may not be isomorphic to the bundle's token system.
peer_reviews.all_missed: Every response treats the bundle as a static artifact transcribed once, but the real recurring cost is re-adopting an updated bundle into an already-edited tokens.css — a diff/merge problem, not a one-time extract — and whether 'preserve oklch, Tailwind off' is even stable across Claude Design releases determines if the mechanical core is a durable script or itself bespoke.

peer_reviews.strongest_why: It alone reframes the problem correctly (tool-handoff), decomposes by repeatability into three jobs with distinct verdicts, and proposes a falsifiable smallest experiment rather than just asserting an answer.
peer_reviews.blind_spot_what: It lands on 'a scoped command, closest to (B)' — conflating a slash command with a mode inside design-system-bootstrap, two materially different artifacts, so its verdict is internally muddled.
peer_reviews.all_missed: None interrogated the central premise that the kit should own the tokens at all — the right move may be to leave the bundle authoritative and have the kit merely reference it, making transcription itself the mistake; and all treat the README as static leverage, none asking what happens to a transcription convention when the README's output format changes underneath it.

peer_reviews.strongest_why: It names the fidelity trap — mapping a 40-token oklch/motion system onto the two-tier shape mutates it, creating two drifting sources of truth — which reframes even the 'mechanical' part as risky, not safely automatable.
peer_reviews.blind_spot_what: It treats token transcription as cleanly deterministic and skill-worthy while ignoring that mapping an external structure onto the kit's shape is itself lossy and judgment-laden, so its 'build the mechanical 20%' verdict rests on a false premise.
peer_reviews.all_missed: Every response argues about whether to build a tool, but none questions the strategic premise that the bundle should be bent into the kit's house format at all — Claude Design ships a working prototype plus its own README, so the highest-value move may be to consume the bundle natively and let the kit govern only what it already owns, making 'adopt' a net-negative translation layer regardless of whether it's a skill, mode, or command.

chairman.recommendation_headline: Don't build a fourth skill — and before building anything, run a one-hour timed hand-transcription of the real bundle to prove the 'mechanical' token-adopt is actually lossless.
chairman.agreement: Near-total convergence on the load-bearing points. (1) A standalone sibling skill (option A) is wrong — no advisor endorses a fourth node; every lens flags it as deepening the kit's headline non-composition weakness with an un-orchestrated entry mode. (2) The proposal decomposes into three jobs of wildly different repeatability: token transcription (parse :root into a two-tier tokens.css, preserve oklch() verbatim, Tailwind off) is the only deterministic part; multi-aesthetic reconciliation is irreducible human judgment; pixel-perfect component recreation is what Claude Design's own README already hands the agent — duplicating it is the trap. (3) 'Extract, don't generate' is mostly a transcription chore, not a skill — the repeatable residue is small (a command, not a skill) and everything past it collapses into bespoke judgment every run. (4) The honest ceiling is low and known: reliable token adoption, advisory everything else. (5) The cheapest way to settle it is empirical: hand-transcribe the real bundle's tokens now and time it.
chairman.clashes: Largely unanimous on direction; two genuine fault lines remain. First: build-the-mechanical-core-now (Architect, Veteran, Long View — a narrowed mode or command) vs. defer-until-bundle-2 (Skeptic, Stranger — D/C). The build camp says the token-parse step earns its keep today because it can't drift or hallucinate a palette; the defer camp says designing any reconciliation procedure from exactly one bundle (N=1) hard-codes that bundle's quirks as 'the format.' Second, and deeper: should the kit ingest the tokens at all? The Skeptic alone pushes the fidelity trap to its conclusion — mapping a 40-token oklch/motion/font system onto the two-tier shape may not be isomorphic, so 'adopt' creates a second, drifting source of truth, strictly worse than using the bundle as-is. A minor muddle: the Veteran's 'scoped command, closest to B' conflates a slash command with a mode inside design-system-bootstrap — two materially different artifacts.
chairman.blind_spots: The peer reviews surfaced gaps every advisor missed or under-weighted. (1) The fidelity / lossy-isomorphism risk (caught by four of five reviewers): even the 'safe' 20% may be lossy. If the two-tier tokens.css cannot losslessly hold the bundle's oklch/motion/font structure, transcription itself is the mistake — it manufactures drift between two sources of truth, and the 'build the mechanical core' verdict rests on that unproven premise. (2) The versioned-bundle re-adoption lifecycle: every advisor treats the bundle as a static artifact transcribed once, but the real recurring cost is re-adopting an updated bundle into an already-edited tokens.css — a diff/merge problem, not a one-time extract — and whether 'preserve oklch, Tailwind off' is even stable across Claude Design releases decides whether the mechanical core is a durable script or itself bespoke. (3) The unexamined premise that the kit should own the tokens at all: Claude Design ships a working prototype plus its own README, so the highest-value move may be to consume the bundle natively and let the kit govern only what it already owns. (4) The Long View's smuggled premise: it justifies mode-over-sibling by appealing to an orchestrator the kit has explicitly not built — possibly a phantom requirement.
chairman.recommendation: Do not build design-handoff-adopt as a fourth sibling skill — that is unanimous and correct. Side with the Architect's decomposition gated by the Skeptic's fidelity test, the strongest combined reasoning. Sequence: (1) Resolve the ingest-at-all question first, empirically. Before writing any artifact, hand-transcribe the real bundle's :root into the kit's two-tier tokens.css and answer one question: is it lossless? If the bundle's oklch/motion/font structure does not fit the two-tier shape without mutation, stop — do not adopt; leave the bundle authoritative, let the kit reference it, and govern only what the kit already owns (write guard, doc structure, rules). A lossy 'adopt' is strictly worse than using the bundle as-is. (2) If and only if transcription is lossless and fast, build the mechanical 20% as a slash command under the write guard — not a skill, not a design-system-bootstrap mode; a command is the smallest artifact and sidesteps both the Veteran's command/mode conflation and the routing-mode debt. Scope it to: parse :root, preserve oklch() verbatim, fold into two-tier tokens.css, flip Tailwind off. Leave reconciliation as an advisory prompt and recreation delegated to the bundle's README in guided prose. (3) Regardless, do not design a reconciliation 'procedure' from this one bundle (N=1 hazard); log what was genuinely mechanical and treat the re-adoption / diff-merge lifecycle as an open question for bundle #2. Net: closer to defer-then-narrow-to-a-command than to building anything now — defer the procedure, but run the cheap test that converts the defer into a confident build-or-don't.
chairman.first_step: Open the real Claude Design bundle and hand-transcribe its :root custom properties into the kit's two-tier tokens.css right now, on a timer. You are testing one thing: does every token (including oklch, motion, font) land losslessly in the two-tier shape? Lossless and under ~30 minutes → build the token-adopt slash command. Lossy or fighting the structure → do not adopt; keep the bundle authoritative and have the kit reference it.

alignment.name: The Skeptic
alignment.argument: Transcription is a 30-line command not a skill, and mapping a 40-token system onto the two-tier shape is lossy — adopt may create a second drifting source of truth.

alignment.name: The Architect
alignment.argument: Decompose into three jobs by repeatability, automate only deterministic token transcription, and run a timed hand-transcription to settle build-vs-defer empirically.

alignment.name: The Stranger
alignment.argument: It's a permanent tool to reformat an artifact that already ships its own README — do the next bundle as a one-off and only then consider a script.

alignment.name: The Long View
alignment.argument: Transcription races a moving target you don't control; the durable asset is the kit's governance layer, and one skill with two entry modes pays down orchestrator debt.

alignment.name: The Veteran
alignment.argument: Build the mechanical 20% as automation under the write guard and leave the judgment-laden 80% as guided prose; it's a command, not a sibling skill.
